incidents.description: A civil lawsuit alleged that more than 240 Target stores throughout California unlawfully handled and disposed of hazardous wastes including pesticides, electronics, pharmaceuticals, and other materials over a seven-year period, resulting in a court-ordered settlement.

California's Attorney General and 24 local government attorneys announced a statewide settlement with Target for improperly disposing of hazardous waste including electronics, batteries, aerosol cans, fluorescent bulbs, medical waste, and confidential medical information between 2012 and 2016, in violation of a 2011 court judgment.

Target was found to have sold non-complying automotive windshield washer fluid, non-complying reed diffuser and room spray air freshener products, and non-complying portable fuel containers in California between 2003 and 2008, resulting in a stipulated settlement with the State of California.

Target sold automotive windshield washer fluid products in California with VOC concentrations exceeding California Air Resources Board standards, resulting in 4.93 tons of excess VOC emissions and a settlement agreement.

Target was found to have distributed and sold confetti string products ('Horrible Spooky String') containing banned hydrochlorofluorocarbons (HCFCs) in violation of the Clean Air Act, and agreed to pay a civil penalty.

The DOJ and EPA announced a prospective purchaser agreement with Target for the cleanup and redevelopment of the Denova Environmental Superfund Site in Rialto, California, under which Target paid the EPA $100,000 and performed cleanup steps in exchange for CERCLA liability protection.
incidents.agency_or_court: U.S. Department of Justice / U.S. Environmental Protection Agency

incidents.description: Target settled a case with the California Air Resources Board for selling 'Splash De-Icer Premium Windshield Washer Fluid -30°F' containing VOC concentrations exceeding California regulatory limits for the Automotive Windshield Washer Fluid category.

Target sold automotive windshield washer fluid (Splash De-icer Premium Windshield Washer Fluid -30°F) in California between December 2013 and February 2014 with VOC concentrations exceeding state standards, resulting in a CARB settlement.

Target was found to have sold uncertified indoor air cleaning devices in California and to have failed to display required advisories on its website for uncertified devices, in violation of the California Air Resources Board Regulation for Limiting Ozone Emissions From Indoor Air Cleaning Devices.

The Environmental Research Center filed a Proposition 65 Notice of Violation against Target Brands, Inc. and Target Corporation alleging that the product 'Vitanica Slow Flow Fast-Acting Support Healthy Menstrual Flow' sold by Target contains lead at levels requiring a Proposition 65 warning without one being provided.

A class action lawsuit was filed against Target in the U.S. District Court for the Central District of California alleging that its Good & Gather private-label tuna products are falsely advertised as 'sustainably caught' despite suppliers allegedly using harmful fishing practices.
incidents.agency_or_court: U.S. District Court for the Central District of California

incidents.description: A recall was initiated for over 25,000 units of Good & Gather Baby Pea, Zucchini, Kale & Thyme Vegetable Puree distributed by Target due to elevated lead contamination levels, classified as a Class II recall.

EPA Region 10 issued a Stop Sale, Use or Removal Order against Target for distributing and selling misbranded Hoover Steam Mop pesticide devices with false and misleading labeling in violation of FIFRA between June and August 2022.

Target agreed to pay $4.6 million to settle a class action alleging it failed to compensate approximately 13,700 warehouse distribution center workers for time spent in mandatory pre- and post-shift security screenings and walking to work stations at three New Jersey warehouses, in violation of the New Jersey Wage and Hour Law and New Jersey Wage Payment Law.
incidents.agency_or_court: U.S. District Court, District of New Jersey (Case No. 23:cv00030)

incidents.description: Target settled a class action alleging its criminal background check hiring practices had a disparate impact on African American and Latino job applicants in violation of Title VII, covering applicants denied employment from 2006 to 2016.
incidents.jurisdiction: Federal – Southern District of New York
incidents.agency_or_court: U.S. District Court for the Southern District of New York

incidents.description: The EEOC found reasonable cause that three pre-hire employment assessments used by Target disproportionately screened out applicants for exempt-level professional positions based on race and sex in violation of Title VII, and that one assessment violated the ADA, resulting in a $2.8 million resolution agreement.

Target agreed to pay $2.225 million to resolve a class action lawsuit claiming it failed to disclose wage scales and salary ranges in Washington state job postings as required by the state's wage transparency law.
Target agreed to pay $1.25 million to settle a proposed class action alleging it failed to provide employees proper meal and rest breaks.

Minnesota Attorney General reached an $800,000 settlement with Shipt, Inc. to resolve a 2022 lawsuit alleging that Shipt misclassified delivery workers as independent contractors rather than employees, to avoid providing employment protections under Minnesota law.

The EEOC secured a settlement requiring Target Corporation to pay for alleged racial harassment in the workplace, including inappropriate comments and verbal abuse directed at employees based on their race.

A consent decree was entered requiring Target to pay $510,000 to four African American applicants denied employment as assistant store managers in its Milwaukee/Madison district based on race, resolving EEOC litigation filed in 2002.
incidents.agency_or_court: U.S. District Court, Eastern District of Wisconsin (Civil Action No. 02-C-0146) / EEOC

incidents.description: Target, Inc. incurred a labor relations violation penalty of $75,000 assessed by the National Labor Relations Board.
Target agreed to pay $45,000 to settle an EEOC disability discrimination lawsuit alleging the company violated the Americans with Disabilities Act.
Target paid $22,450 in damages to nearly 70 employees at its South Philadelphia store for violating the Philadelphia Fair Workweek Law by failing to post schedules at least two weeks in advance.

incidents.entity: Target - N Ca Distribution Center T-555
incidents.description: OSHA cited Target's Northern California Distribution Center T-555 for two violations totaling $18,600 in penalties for significant safety violations found at the worksite.
incidents.agency_or_court: U.S. Occupational Safety and Health Administration

incidents.description: OSHA cited Target for a serious entanglement hazard at a facility after employee clothing contacted a rotating conveyor shaft, with the formal settlement penalty set at $8,096.40 following a contested reduction from an initial $13,494.00 penalty.
A law firm began investigating whether Target violated the WARN Act by failing to provide 60 days' notice before a mass layoff of 528 employees at its Minneapolis facility.
incidents.agency_or_court: Minnesota Department of Employment and Economic Development / Strauss Borrelli PLLC investigation

incidents.description: 47 state attorneys general and the District of Columbia reached an $18.5 million multistate settlement with Target over the 2013 data breach that affected approximately 40 million payment card accounts and over 60 million customers.
incidents.agency_or_court: Multistate Attorneys General (47 states and DC)

incidents.description: A class action settlement was reached and granted final court approval resolving dozens of consolidated consumer lawsuits arising from the 2013 Target data breach affecting customers who used payment cards or provided contact information at Target stores between November 27 and December 18, 2013.
incidents.agency_or_court: U.S. Federal Court (In re Target Corp. Customer Data Security Breach Litigation)

incidents.description: Target settled a class action (Garcia v. Target Corporation) for $7.05 million over allegations that the company placed automated debt collection calls to consumers who had not consented to receive them between March 2012 and May 2018.
incidents.agency_or_court: U.S. District Court, District of Minnesota

incidents.description: Target settled a class action lawsuit filed by the National Federation of the Blind, which alleged that Target's website was inaccessible to blind users in violation of the ADA, agreeing to pay $6 million in damages to the California class.
incidents.agency_or_court: U.S. District Court, Northern District of California

incidents.description: Target Enterprise, Inc. agreed to a nearly $2.3 million class action settlement to resolve allegations of illegal debt collection practices affecting Massachusetts residents.
Target agreed to pay a $600,000 civil penalty to the Consumer Product Safety Commission for importing and selling toys with illegally high concentrations of lead paint.

An Associated Press investigation found that Target carried food products in its supply chain produced by U.S. prison labor, where workers receive wages as low as 52 cents per hour and lack standard health and safety protections.

A coalition of investors sent a formal letter to Target's Board of Directors demanding clarification on steps taken after a New York Times investigation alleged that Target's U.S. suppliers may have illegally employed underage migrant children as young as 13 years old.

China Labor Watch investigated Target supplier Rushan Alice Garments Company and found systematic violations of Chinese labor law and Target's own vendor standards, including extreme overtime hours, insufficient overtime pay, and use of subcontractors with worse conditions, with Target's own audit reportedly finding no infractions.

China Labor Watch alleged that three Target supplier factories in mainland China — Ningbo Lucky Craft Co, Hangzhou Ownseas Pen Co, and Dongguan Fuxiang Garment Co — employed workers as young as 15 years old for 11–13 hour days under dangerous conditions.

Target did not respond to allegations from Business & Human Rights Resource Centre regarding child labour practices in its supply chain as reported by an independent investigation into vendor hiring practices.

The DOJ and EPA announced a prospective purchaser agreement with Target for the cleanup and redevelopment of the Denova Environmental Superfund Site in Rialto, California, under which Target paid the EPA $100,000 and performed cleanup steps in exchange for CERCLA liability protection.
incidents.agency_or_court: U.S. Department of Justice / U.S. Environmental Protection Agency

incidents.description: Target was found to have distributed and sold confetti string products ('Horrible Spooky String') containing banned hydrochlorofluorocarbons (HCFCs) in violation of the Clean Air Act, and agreed to pay a civil penalty.

Target was found to have sold non-complying automotive windshield washer fluid, non-complying reed diffuser and room spray air freshener products, and non-complying portable fuel containers in California between 2003 and 2008, resulting in a stipulated settlement with the State of California.

A civil lawsuit alleged that more than 240 Target stores throughout California unlawfully handled and disposed of hazardous wastes including pesticides, electronics, pharmaceuticals, and other materials over a seven-year period, resulting in a court-ordered settlement.

Target sold automotive windshield washer fluid (Splash De-icer Premium Windshield Washer Fluid -30°F) in California between December 2013 and February 2014 with VOC concentrations exceeding state standards, resulting in a CARB settlement.
incidents._merge_note: Merged with near-duplicate dated 2016-03-09 (same product, same amount, same agency). The 2016-03-09 record's date appears to be the settlement execution date for the 2013-2014 violation period; canonical record uses the violation period start date (2014-01-01) and the official settlement PDF as source. The 2016-03-09 entry is retired as duplicate.

gaps.note: Only two incidents recorded across a three-year span despite Target operating hundreds of California stores; likely reflects limited enforcement visibility or unreported violations during this early period.
No incidents recorded during this period; may reflect enforcement slowdowns during the COVID-19 pandemic or a lag between violations and formal actions.

related_clusters.label: California CARB VOC / Windshield Washer Fluid violations
Good & Gather product safety concerns (lead & sustainability)
Federal EPA enforcement actions (HCFC, FIFRA, Superfund)

subsidy_after_violation.note: Target received a 2011 court settlement for hazardous waste disposal. A 2018 follow-on settlement of $7,400,000 was required because Target violated the 2011 court judgment between 2012 and 2016, indicating ongoing non-compliance after the original enforcement action. No subsidies are documented in the incident record.

net_public_transfer.math_note: Total documented penalties/settlements: $100,000 (2004 Superfund agreement) + $120,000 (2007 Clean Air Act fine) + $500,000 (2009 California VOC settlement) + $22,500,000 (2011 hazardous waste settlement) + $58,300 (2014/2016 CARB settlement) + $7,400,000 (2018 hazardous waste settlement) + $221,850 (2019 CARB settlement) + $6,691 (2025 CARB settlement) = $30,906,841. No subsidy amounts are documented in the incident record, so net public transfer cannot be calculated.

regulatory_non_enforcement.agency: Alameda County Superior Court / California Attorney General
regulatory_non_enforcement.summary: Target violated a 2011 court judgment requiring proper hazardous waste disposal by continuing to improperly dispose of hazardous waste (electronics, batteries, aerosol cans, fluorescent bulbs, medical waste, and confidential medical information) from 2012 to 2016, resulting in a second settlement of $7,400,000 in 2018. The original enforcement action did not prevent recurrence over a multi-year period.

Target was found to have sold non-complying VOC products in California across multiple separate enforcement actions (2009, 2014/2016, and 2019), suggesting repeated violations of California air quality regulations for consumer products despite prior settlements and awareness of compliance requirements.

EPA issued a Stop Sale, Use or Removal Order against Target in 2023 for distributing and selling misbranded pesticide devices with false and misleading labeling in violation of FIFRA. No monetary penalty amount was documented, suggesting the enforcement action may not have resulted in financial consequences beyond the sales halt.

summaries.one_line: Target Corporation is a U.S. retail chain with a documented record of environmental regulatory violations across multiple jurisdictions.
summaries.worker_summary: No labor, wage, discrimination, or union incidents are documented in the available incident record for Target Corporation. The institutional data reviewed does not include findings related to wage theft, workplace discrimination, collective bargaining disputes, or occupational safety enforcement actions.
summaries.community_summary: Target has accumulated over $30.9 million in documented environmental penalties and settlements between 2004 and 2025, primarily in California. Violations include improper disposal of hazardous waste—such as pesticides, pharmaceuticals, electronics, batteries, and medical waste—at more than 240 store locations over a seven-year period, resulting in a $22.5 million settlement in 2011 and a subsequent $7.4 million settlement in 2018 after Target continued the same conduct in violation of the original court judgment. Separate enforcement actions by the California Air Resources Board document repeated sales of consumer products with volatile organic compound concentrations exceeding state standards across multiple settlement cycles (2009, 2014/2016, 2019, and 2025). A 2007 EPA fine concerned the sale of products containing banned ozone-depleting substances. A 2023 EPA Stop Sale Order addressed misbranded pesticide device labeling. A 2025 FDA Class II recall covered a private-label baby food product due to elevated lead contamination. Two additional matters—a false sustainability advertising class action and a Proposition 65 lead-exposure notice—were pending as of the record date.
summaries.public_transfer_note: Total documented penalties and settlements paid by Target amount to approximately $30.9 million; no subsidy amounts are recorded in the available incident data, so a net public transfer figure cannot be calculated.
